summary: Kenneth teaches a systematic method for finding stocks suitable for covered call selling by cherry-picking mid-cap growth ETF holdings, calculating premium-to-share-price ratios, and identifying stocks that are affordable yet generate meaningful premiums. The core principle is seeking volatile stocks with high premiums while avoiding mega-cap names like Apple and Google that are expensive and low-premium.
takeaways: Target stocks with 1-3% weekly premium yield (divide premium by share price, multiply by 100)
Anything over 3% premium indicates high-risk/unusual activity (earnings, etc.)
Sell covered calls at out-of-the-money strike prices for highest premium possible
Require minimum 100 shares per contract to execute covered calls
Use mid-cap growth ETFs as sourcing tool, cherry-pick top 10 holdings for individual stock research
Avoid Magnificent Seven (Google, Nvidia, Apple, Meta) - too expensive per share, low premiums
Select stocks with lower share price (<$1,500 per 100 shares) for retail accessibility

strategy_candidates.change: Add mid-cap growth ETF cherry-picking as systematic sourcing pipeline for covered call candidates to replace ad-hoc stock selection
strategy_candidates.evidence: Kenneth demonstrates using Seeking Alpha to filter mid-cap growth ETFs, then researching their top 10 holdings to find affordable, high-premium stocks. This provides a repeatable screening process more efficient than intuition-based selection, directly applicable to Brian's $50k Premium Desk targeting $1,250/mo premium across multiple positions.

title: Find stocks for covered calls
